Part 2 of 2 — Why They Killed It (And Not for the Reason You Think)
Press enter or click to view image in full size

String templatesIn Part 1 we watched Java fall for String Templates: interpolation at last, but ambitious — \{} holes, pluggable processors, and the genuinely beautiful idea that a template is data (fragments and values held apart) and a processor is an interpreter that folds it into any type, including an injection-proof PreparedStatement. Two previews, broad approval, a finalizing JEP in motion. And then, twelve weeks from the JDK 23 finish line, the OpenJDK team withdrew it outright — the first feature ever pulled from Java's preview pipeline.
The internet blamed the syntax. Let’s do the autopsy.
It wasn’t the syntax
Yes, people complained about \{...} instead of $, and about that STR. dot in front of the literal that the JEP itself described, with almost comic understatement, as just "a dot character, as seen in other kinds of expressions." Those complaints were real. They were also not the reason, and treating them as the reason misses the actually-interesting thing.
The real reason, stated plainly in the OpenJDK issue that removed the feature, is that the processor-centric design was confusing to users and lacked compositionality. Sit with both halves of that, because each is a distinct problem.
